title: Thursday assorted links - Marginal REVOLUTION
gist: In this link-roundup post, the one substantive item is Samuel Hammond's argument (relayed by Tyler Cowen) that the AI boom embarrasses progressive economic commentators by showing financialization was a self-resolving macro story and that growth is being driven by AI rather than a hoped-for green transition; Cowen adds that it also vindicates the internet/social-media era and shows many tech leaders are genuinely capable rather than lucky.

themes.theme: AI boom vs. the financialization/green-transition narrative

insights.claim: Samuel Hammond argues the AI boom is doubly upsetting to progressive economic commentators because it shows that 'financialization' of the economy was largely a macro story that eventually resolved itself on its own, and because current growth is being driven by AI rather than the green transition progressives had hoped would be the growth engine.
insights.speaker: Samuel Hammond
insights.so_what: Reframes the current AI-driven growth cycle as evidence against the progressive economic critique of financialization and against green-industrial-policy-led growth theses -- relevant for positioning around which growth narrative (AI capex vs. green transition) actually drives macro outcomes.
insights.quote: The AI boom is doubly upsetting to progressive economic commentators because it a) shows “financialization” was largely a macro story that eventually solved itself, and b) the boom is being driven by AI rather than their hoped-for “green transition”.

insights.claim: Tyler Cowen adds that the AI boom also demonstrates the internet and social media era was genuinely worth it economically, and that many tech companies are run by geniuses rather than superficial idiots.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: A vindication-of-Big-Tech-leadership take -- signals the author's high conviction in incumbent tech management quality, relevant context for evaluating skepticism toward large tech company execution.
insights.quote: Plus it shows the internet and social media really were worth it, and that many tech companies are run by geniuses not superficial idiots.
insights.locator: Plus it shows the internet and social media
